coredata.dc:title: Luminescent graphene quantum dots from oxidized multi-walled carbon nanotubes
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2012 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Minati, L.
Torrengo, S.
Maniglio, D.
Migliaresi, C.
Speranza, G.
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent nano-graphene quantum dots (GQD) were isolated from oxidized carbon nanotube suspension with the aid of cysteamine. The oxidized GQD were thiol functionalized with cysteamine in presence of dicyclohexylcarbodiimide as coupling agent. The GQD chemistry and morphology were characterized by means of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and atomic force microscopy. The thiolated graphene quantum dots exhibit an intense luminescence (quantum yield around 10%) in the visible range with an excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanostructures
Photoluminescence spectroscopy
Photoelectron spectroscopy
Optical properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Top: Scheme of the isolation and functionalization of GQD from oxidized carbon nanotubes. Bottom left: Absorption spectrum of thio-GQD in DMF. Inset: Photo taken under visible light. Bottom right: Photoluminescence spectra acquired at different excitation wavelengths (from 380 to 560 with 20 nm step). Inset: Photo of the thio-GQD in DMF suspension irradiated with a 390 nm lamp.

Left: XPS C1s core line of oxidized (black) and thiolated (red) GQD. Inset: Snapshot of the carboxylic-amide region. Right: S2p XPS spectrum of thiolated GQD. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Left: Atomic force microscopy image of thio-GQD deposited on a mica substrate. Right: Heights distribution. Inset: Cross-section analysis along the line as indicated in the AFM image on the left.

head.ce:title: Luminescent graphene quantum dots from oxidized multi-walled carbon nanotubes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: FBK, Via Sommarive 18, Povo, 38123 Trento, Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Engineering and Industrial Technologies Department, University of Trento, Via Mesiano 77, 38123 Trento, Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Biotech Research Center, University of Trento, Via delle Regole 101, Mattarello, 38123 Trento, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent nano-graphene quantum dots (GQD) were isolated from oxidized carbon nanotube suspension with the aid of cysteamine. The oxidized GQD were thiol functionalized with cysteamine in presence of dicyclohexylcarbodiimide as coupling agent. The GQD chemistry and morphology were characterized by means of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and atomic force microscopy. The thiolated graphene quantum dots exhibit an intense luminescence (quantum yield around 10%) in the visible range with an excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence.

► Multi-wall carbon nanotubes were oxidized by strong acids. ► Graphene quantum dots were isolated from carbon nanotubes. ► Graphene quantum dots were thiol functionalized by cysteamine. ► Graphene quantum dots exhibit an intense luminescence in the visible.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the optical properties of carbon nanostructures (carbon nanotubes, carbon dots, nano-graphene, nanodiamonds etc.) were deeply investigated by the scientific community [1–5]. Potential applications of these nanostructures in nanoelectronics, photonics and bio-imaging pushed the attention towards the synthesis of new highly fluorescent carbon-based nanoparticles. Moreover the possibility to functionalize the carbon surface via covalent bonding with different molecules allows the synthesis of carbon nanostructures with a controlled solubility in different solvents. Since the first report presented by Riggs [6] many different carbon nanostructures and synthesis procedures were found to obtain luminescent nanocarbons.

These include top-down approaches, such as laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation of graphite and carbon nanotubes, oxidation of candle soot [1,7–11] as well as bottom-up methods such as the chemical reduction of carbonaceous compounds [12–14]. Both the approaches induce the formation of functional groups on the surface of the carbon nanostructures. In particular it was reported the presence of carboxyl, hydroxyl, carbonyl and ether groups leading to visible luminescence which is very interesting for many applications.

The radiative mechanism is nowadays still an object of controversy but the defect states introduced by the functionalization seem to be the principal responsible of the luminescence in the visible range [6]. This is a consequence of the deep change of the electronic structure near the Fermi level induced by the oxidizing process. It was reported that an increase of the nanostructure luminescence may be obtained through an optimization of the nanoparticle surface functionalization through appropriate passivation agents [15]. In addition the functionalization of carbon dots with inorganic salts has increased the quantum yield up to 78% [16] a value similar to the best semiconductor quantum dots present in literature, making these systems highly appetible for biological applications including bio-markers, fluorescent labels and drug delivery systems. The main advantages of the carbon-based systems respect to semiconductor quantum dots are their lower toxicity and absence of photoblinking. In addition, carbon nanostructures do not suffer of photobleaching that limits the optical performance of the organic dyes [17,18].

Graphene Quantum Dots (GQD) represent a new class of carbon nanomaterials with interesting optical properties. Differently to the carbon dots that display a spherical shape GQD are composed by small sheets of graphene with a “quasi-2D” structure. Graphene Quantum Dots were typically prepared through strong oxidation, hydrothermal treatments or electrochemical treatment of carbonaceous sources like graphite, graphene oxide and carbon fibers [19,20].

Herein we report a new method for the high yield isolation of strongly photoluminescent GQD from oxidized carbon nanotubes (ox-CNT). The oxidized GQD (ox-GQD) were converted to thiol-functionalized GQD (thio-GQD) in presence of a coupling agent. The prepared GQD possess a photoluminescence quantum yield in dimethylformamide (DMF) equal to 10.3%.

Multi-walled carbon nanotubes (0.1 g, Nanoamor USA) were cut and oxidized in a 10 ml (1:3) HNO3/H2SO4 acid mixture, followed by ultrasonication for 6 h at 40 °C. The black suspension was then diluted with water and allowed to stand overnight for precipitation. After the removal of the supernatant the suspension of ox-CNT was diluted with deionised water and filtered with a 0.05 μm filter membrane under vacuum. The ox-CNT were found to form stable colloidal suspensions in a plenty of solvents including water, ethanol and dimethylformamide. Cysteamine solution (1 mL of 1 mM ethanolic solution) was added to the suspension and the CNT were mixed for 24 h and then decanted overnight.
After 24 h the black CNT precipitate was removed by filtration using a 200 nm Teflon filter to eliminate the CNTs and the filtrate was collected (ox-GQD).

Dicyclohexylcarbodiimide solution in DMF (10 mM) was added to start the reaction. The suspension was mixed at room temperature for 24 h. The GQD were finally purified by repeated filtration through a cut-off membrane (MW 1500) and rinsed with fresh DMF (thio-GQD).

X-ray photoelectron spectra were acquired by using a Scienta ESCA 200 analyzer (Gammadata, Sweden) equipped with a monochromatized Al Kα X-ray source. Core lines were acquired at 150 eV pass energy that corresponds to an energy resolution of 0.30 eV. All spectra were aligned fixing the C1s deriving from carbon nanotubes at 284.40 eV.

Absorption measurements were performed using a UV–vis–near infrared spectrophotometer (Cary 5000) in dual beam mode. Luminescence spectra were recorded using a Xe lamp as excitation source coupled to a single grating monochromator, in a spectral range extending from 380 to 560 nm. The fluorescence from the solution was corrected taking into account the intensity of the excitation wavelengths measured with a photodiode placed in the excitation light beam close to the sample. Raman scattering measurements were performed in a wide wavenumber range between 300 and 3000 cm−1 by means of a microprobe set-up (Horiba-Jobin-Yvon, model Aramis) consisting of a He–Ne laser operating at 633 nm (power 20 mW), a narrowband edge filter, a 46 cm focal length spectrograph using a 1800 grooves/mm holographic grating and a charge-coupled device (CCD) detector. The laser beam was focused onto the sample surface with a spot size of about 1 μm using a 100× objective with numerical aperture NA = 0.9. The resolution was about 0.35 cm−1 pixel−1. Drops of the GQD suspension were deposited and dried onto a carbon-coated copper grid. Samples were observed using a Philips CM12 TEM operated at 120 kV and equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDXS). Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) measurements were acquired with an NT-MDT Solver Pro in semi contact mode using epitaxially grown tips (typical curvature 1 nm, typical elastic constant 5.5 N m−1).

The G-band represents the sp2 hybridized stretching mode of carbon bonds. The D mode is classically attributed to structural defects on the graphitic plane. The spectrum of ox-GQD is very similar to that reported by other authors [28] and is significant broader respect to that of oxidized carbon nanotubes (Fig. S1a, red line) and that of short carbon nanotubes that we presented recently [29]. Differently, Raman spectrum of the thio-GQD sample shows a broad luminescence band that masks the phonon transitions (Fig. S1b). The XPS analysis of the ox-GQD shows the appearance of features on the C1s core line at around 288.80 eV assigned to the formation of carboxylic groups.
In thio-GQD the C1s peak at 288.80 eV (carboxylic bond) shifts to 288.23 eV (amide bond), confirming the high efficiency of the functionalization.

The XPS quantitative analysis on the thio-GQD reveals high amount of sulphur (3.27 at.% see Table S2). The analysis of the S2p region shows the classic two peaks core line with the main peak (S2p3/2) placed at 163.34 eV in agreement with the binding energy values of thiol groups.

Atomic Force Microscopy analysis reported in Fig. 3 shows that the luminescent dots have an average height around 1.2 nm (associated to a bi- or tri-layered graphene structure). The average dimension calculated by TEM is around 4–5 nm (Fig. S3). The fluorescent carbon dots are likely to be composed by carbon nanotubes fragments formed during the acid oxidation. The dimension of the carbon nanostructures is very similar to that reported by other groups in the literature [19]. Scanning Electron Microscopy analysis of the GQD suspension does not indicate the presence of tubular structures typical of the carbon nanotubes in the sample (data not shown). It seems reasonable to attribute the fluorescence of the suspension only to the GQD.

It is worth noting that the remaining purified carbon nanotubes do not show any luminescence indicating that the carbon dots could be the main responsible of the CNTs luminescence reported in our previous paper [30] where the visible emission of the suspension was attributed to the CNT luminescence. The ox-GQD display a lower luminescence intensity respect to the thio-GQD with a quantum yield of about 2–3%.

In addition, the luminescence spectra of ox-GQD appear broader and with a more structured lineshape respect to the thio-GQD (Fig. S4). This indicates that the passivation of the oxidized groups by the cysteamine molecules modifies the electronic structures of the GQD near the Fermi level.

The GQD could not be reasonably associated to small carbonaceous debris (also called fulvic acids) isolated from oxidized carbon nanotubes by some authors [31–33]. Because of their high lateral size (around 4–5 nm) these systems are likely to be composed by hundreds of aromatic rings distorted by the presence of the functional groups. The GQD are more similar to the fluorescent nanoparticles isolated by electrophoretic purification of nitric acid-treated single wall carbon nanotubes as reported by Xu et al. [34].

Differently from other works reported in the literature the GQD were obtained from oxidized carbon nanotubes by an easy and low cost process without needing high temperature or sophisticated equipments. In addition, the isolation of fluorescent GQD from functionalized CNT opens the possibility to investigate and clarify the origin of the photoluminescence in functionalized CNTs.

Nano-graphene quantum dots were efficiently isolated by oxidized carbon nanotubes suspension with the aid of cysteamine molecules. This procedure allows both the isolation of a suspension of purified carbon nanotubes and a quantitative recovery of carbon dots with interesting optical properties. The intense fluorescence of this material in the visible range makes it an excellent candidate for numerous applications in photonics, optoelectronics and bio-imaging. In addition, due to their intense luminescence and their high solubility in polar solvents like dimethylformamide and ethanol, thio-GQD can be of significant interest for applications such as phosphor in UV-based light emitting diodes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was performed in the framework of the Nanosmart-PAT research project.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2012.08.071.
